abstract: While existing theories typically attribute intraparty competition to electoral systems and formal internal rules, this article delves deeper into these dynamics by analyzing the strategic expression of policy preferences. I argue that while electoral defeats often lead to increased preference heterogeneity within a party, candidates who deviate significantly from the party’s median position are penalized with fewer intraparty votes. Furthermore, the findings suggest that party members grant candidates more flexibility to balance competing policy demands when the party holds government office. To test these hypotheses, this study introduces a novel database of party congress speeches from Germany and France, employing automated text classification to estimate the relative preferences of individual speakers. The results demonstrate that party congress rhetoric offers critical insights into internal power struggles and actor motivations. Ultimately, the evidence reveals a sophisticated interplay between governing status, economic conditions, and the degree of intraparty disagreement.
